title: Chelex-based DNA extraction protocol for insect museum vouchers
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"56hof","text":"Museum biorepositories preserve precious material for scientific research in several fields such as biodiversity, phylogenetics, population genetics, toxicology, environmental monitoring and pathology.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e9686","text":"About half of the 8.7 million described species on Earth are insects and harbor a diverse community of microorganisms representing different association types, such as parasitic, mutualistic, and commensal.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"24h21","text":"Voucher specimens may play an important role in tracking insect-microbiome associations.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4bgt8","text":"Catalog records and voucher specimens are the primary source for: (1) tracking past associations and inferring evolutionary pathways; (2) documenting associations in no longer accessible collection sites; (3) revising the taxonomic status of the associates; (4) resampling the associations over time when new technologies become available.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"60kjm","text":"Here we present a non-destructive, fast, inexpensive, non-toxic protocol for DNA extraction from insect museum specimens with the aim to obtain high quality nucleic acid and sufficient yield for vector-borne pathogens detection, quantification and characterization.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Transfer the the ethanol-preserved Sample on a piece of paper towel to allow the ethanol to evaporate for a few minutes. For dry insect voucher, re-hydrate the Sample in a humidifying chamber Overnight
Place Sample in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube containing 500 µL ethanol 90 % (v/v) for 00:02:00 .
Discard the ethanol and add 500 µL bleach 2.5 % (v/v) (NaOCl - The Clorox Co., Oakland, CA, USA) for 00:05:00 at 7.5 °C . The insect body must be fully immersed in the bleach solution.
Discard bleach solution and rinse 3 X with 250 µL of sterile water, discard the water each time.

Pipet 50 µL TES buffer and 2 µL Proteinase K ( 10 mg/mL , Qiagen) into the tube. Prepare TES buffer (Tris-HCl 20 millimolar (mM), pH 8.0 , EDTA 10 millimolar (mM) , SDS 0.5 % (v/v) ) For 10 mL TES add 0.2 mL EDTA 0.5 Molarity (M) , 0.5 mL SDS 10 % (v/v) , 0.2 mL Tris-HCl 1 Molarity (M) , 9.1 mL water (MilliQ)
Place the Sample into a fresh 1.5 mL centrifuge tube.

Centrifugate the sample for a few seconds to spin down the liquid. With a pipet, transfer 50 µL of the liquid sample into a fresh tube. Store the Sample as appropriate for further morphological studies.
Place a flask containing a 25 Mass / % volume Chelex 100 suspension on a magnetic agitator and mix.
Pipet 40 µL of Chelex directly from the agitator into the tube with the sample.
Incubate the tube at 55 °C for 00:30:00 . Do not shake the tube.
Allow the solution to cool down to Room temperature .
Transfer the supernatant into a fresh 1.5 mL centrifuge tube. The present protocol do not include toxic chemicals
Prepare 25 Mass / % volume Chelex 100 suspension. Add 2.5 g Chelex 100 resin into 10 mL water (MilliQ or NANOpure)
creator: Valeria Trivellone
